title: Dead Drop Signal
shortDescription: Self-deleting executables appear across research labs simultaneously — no malware detected, no data stolen. Someone is testing a delivery system.

briefing.incidentSummary:
An internal research campus reports strange executable files appearing across multiple labs.
The files look harmless and imitate normal internal tools.
They appear without user action and vanish quickly.

No data is stolen. No systems are damaged.
Security software reports no malware.

Yet the files appear at the exact same time.
They execute within the same second.
They delete themselves on a strict timer.

Network activity looks like data theft — but nothing ever leaves the campus.

Someone is testing a delivery system.
Not attacking yet.
Preparing for something larger.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Uncover what these files are and how they communicate

› Identify who is behind the signal

› Analyze binary patterns, metadata timing, and IP routing

› Collect at least 3 correct evidence items

› Flag the hidden operator to close the case

suspects.name: Internal Administrator
suspects.profileNotes: Has high access but lacks advanced signal design skills. Could deploy files across labs but cannot explain the synchronized timing or the dead-drop routing architecture. Suspicion moderate, weakens after IP analysis.

suspects.name: Security Researcher
suspects.profileNotes: Understands dead-drop systems. Careful and methodical. Has partial access and high technical skill. However, no evidence links them directly to infrastructure control. Could be running an independent experiment.

suspects.name: External Attacker
suspects.profileNotes: Initial assumption by security team. Eliminated through IP analysis — all traffic stays internal. Classic false lead to test the player's assumption habits.

suspects.name: Hidden Operator
suspects.profileNotes: Designed the relay logic and controls internal routing. Preparing a command delivery system without triggering security alerts. The dead-drop approach means no payload has been deployed yet — but the infrastructure is ready. Matches all evidence.

minigame.title: Bypass Hacker Terminal
minigame.hint: Crack the 3-character exfiltration node access code.
hints: The first character is a number between 1–9.
The second character is a hex letter (A–F).

onSuccess.message: Terminal breached. Operator identity record is now unlocked.

items.label: sim_cache_v2.exe
items.detail: Malware analysis report on sim_cache_v2.exe recovered from /labA/temp/. File size: 847KB. Digital signature: forged — certificate chain leads to a revoked intermediate CA. The binary contains two functional modules: a legitimate-looking simulation cache wrapper (used as cover) and a hidden exfiltration payload. The payload scans for files matching *.sim, *.dat, and *.cfg within the /research/ directory tree, compresses them using zlib, encrypts with AES-128, and transmits via HTTPS POST to endpoint research-sync.net/upload. The C2 domain was registered 3 weeks before the file appeared. The binary was compiled on a Windows 10 machine; the PDB path embedded in the binary reads 'C:\Users\NV_workstation\projects\simexfil\'. The initials NV match Nalini V.

items.label: render_test.exe
items.detail: Malware analysis report on render_test_patch.exe recovered from /labC/bin/. File size: 851KB — 4KB larger than sim_cache_v2.exe. Binary diff shows the two files share 94% identical code structure — same compiler, same obfuscation pattern, same C2 endpoint. The 4KB difference contains lab-specific path configurations targeting /labC/'s rendering simulation output directory. File creation timestamp: identical to the second as sim_cache_v2.exe in Lab A — both were deployed simultaneously from a single automated dropper script. The file's last-access time was 6 minutes after creation, suggesting the exfiltration ran immediately. Lab C's rendering simulation contained 14 months of proprietary fluid dynamics models.

items.label: calc_patch.exe
items.detail: Malware analysis report on calc_update_v3.exe recovered from /labB/tools/. Third file in the coordinated deployment set. Creation timestamp matches the other two files to the second — all three dropped simultaneously. This file targeted /labB/'s structural calculation output directory, containing finite element analysis results for classified material stress tests. The binary's import table includes calls to WinHTTP for the same C2 endpoint. Notably, this file also contains a persistence mechanism: it added itself to the Windows startup registry key HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run under the name 'CalcToolUpdater'. This indicates it was intended to survive reboots and continue exfiltration over multiple sessions.

rows.value: Identical opcode base with sim_cache and render_test

minigame.title: Timeline Sequence Logic
minigame.description: Reorder file events across all three labs. Convert time gaps into signal patterns and detect repeating cycles.
minigame.hint: If three files on three different machines are created, executed, and deleted at the exact same second — that is not coincidence.
hints: Line up the create, execute, and delete timestamps for all three files.
The gap between creation and execution is always 2 seconds. The gap to deletion is always 90 seconds.
Check the server logs for previous weeks — this pattern repeats every 7 days.

onSuccess.message: IP Traces now accessible. Weekly test cycle confirmed — this is rehearsal, not attack.

items.label: sim_cache_v2.exe — Metadata
items.detail: Created, executed, and deleted at times perfectly synchronized with the other two lab files. The 2-second gap between creation and execution and the 90-second auto-delete are consistent across all three machines.

items.label: render_test.exe — Metadata
items.detail: Identical timestamps to sim_cache_v2.exe despite being on a different machine in a different lab. Synchronized to UTC — not local system time. Deletion fully automated.

items.label: Previous Week Log Comparison
items.detail: Server logs reveal this exact pattern occurred 7 days prior and 14 days prior. A weekly on/off signal cycle — the attacker is testing delivery reliability on a regular schedule.

items.label: Lab Manager Access During Window
items.detail: The lab manager (Dr. K. Verma) had badge access to all three labs on the night the files appeared. He entered Lab A at 22:14, Lab B at 22:31, and Lab C at 22:48 — covering the deployment window. However, badge access logs show he was in each lab for under 3 minutes — routine night rounds. The malware was deployed remotely via an authenticated session, not physically introduced via USB. Physical presence is a red herring.
items.irrelevantReason: The malware was deployed via authenticated remote session — not a physical USB drop. The lab manager's physical presence is coincidental.

minigame.title: Network Route Tracing
minigame.description: Connect network nodes, eliminate fake exits, and identify the dead-drop relay point.
minigame.hint: Traffic that never leaves the campus but mimics external routing is designed to fool network monitors.
hints: Trace each hop. Does any traffic actually leave the campus IP range?
The final node (172.16.0.7) has no external destination — it terminates internally.
A dead-drop relay stores and relays commands without a live connection to the operator.

onSuccess.message: Suspect Profiles unlocked. Dead-drop relay confirmed. Threat levels updated.

items.label: Internal Relay
items.detail: Traffic originates from Lab A (10.4.22.18) and routes to internal research proxy 10.4.9.3. No external hop. The routing path mimics exfiltration behavior but all nodes are internal.

items.label: Dead-Drop Node
items.detail: Final routing node 172.16.0.7 receives all traffic but has no onward destination. This is the dead-drop relay — it stores command instructions and holds them until the operator retrieves them without a live connection.

timeline.title: Synchronized Execution
timeline.description: All three files execute within the same second across separate machines.

timeline.title: Network Traffic Initiates
timeline.description: Internal relay traffic begins — mimics exfiltration pattern.

timeline.title: Files Self-Delete
timeline.description: All three executables delete themselves on exact timer.

timeline.title: Previous Cycles Discovered
timeline.description: Same pattern found in server logs from prior weeks. Weekly test cycle confirmed.

perfect.title: Signal Traced
perfect.subtitle: The executables were command containers testing a dead-drop delivery system. No payload deployed — yet.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Partially Resolved
partial.subtitle: Correct operator identified but insufficient evidence to confirm the delivery system.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Accusation
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The routing pattern was internal, not external. The false exit node was a decoy. Wrong assumption ended the investigation.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

coldCase.title: Case Gone Cold
coldCase.subtitle: No evidence collected. The delivery system continues its test cycles undetected.
coldCase.label: COLD CASE

aria.welcomeToHub:
...Signal detected.
Files that appear and vanish. Execution without a user. Deletion on a timer.
This is not an accident. Someone is rehearsing.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Three panels. Three layers of the same pattern.
Start with the files — they're the surface.
The truth is in what they have in common.
aria.viewEvidence:
You're looking at the right thing.
Same second. Different labs. That's not coincidence.
Mark everything that synchronizes.
aria.markEvidence:
Good. You're building the chain.
The files are containers — not tools.
Find out what they're carrying.
aria.decryptionHint:
The metadata holds the cycle.
Compare the timestamps across all three machines.
If creation, execution, and deletion are identical — someone planned this.
aria.flagSuspect:
You have the routing map.
The dead-drop node is the key.
Who has the infrastructure to build this inside the campus?
